summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's appeal of the EU's March ruling on interoperability requirements under the Digital Markets Act, arguing that the mandate goes far beyond reasonable regulation. He distinguishes between legitimate interoperability mandates like USB-C (based on open standards) and the EU's requirement that Apple essentially open its proprietary technologies like AirDrop to third parties. Gruber contends the EU is treating Apple's intellectual property as a public utility rather than promoting actual open standards. He predicts Apple will respond not by opening everything up, but by withholding features from EU customers entirely, as already seen with iPhone Mirroring's absence in Europe.
key_points: The EU's interoperability mandate requires Apple to expose proprietary technologies like AirDrop to third parties, which is fundamentally different from mandating open standards like USB-C or RCS
Third-party system software extensions might actually need broader privileges than Apple's own devices have, creating privacy and security risks
Apple has two compliance paths: open everything to third parties, or limit its own devices in the EU to only what open standards support
iPhone Mirroring's continued absence in the EU is an early signal of Apple choosing the feature-withholding path
The DMA interoperability rules target only Apple and no other company, which Apple explicitly highlights in its statement
money_quotes: These requirements will also hand data-hungry companies sensitive information, which poses massive privacy and security risks to our EU users.

Mandating the inclusion of a standardized port or standardized protocol is the sort of thing government regulatory bodies do. That's very different than if the EC had regulated port compatibility by requiring Apple to open up Lightning.
The EC's March mandate basically says that third-party devices must be permitted to do everything Apple's own devices do when it comes to communicating or interoperating with iPhones and iPads.
Apple will limit its own devices in the EU and only in the EU to the same features available to third-party devices through open standards like Bluetooth.
If AirDrop were brand new, users in the EU wouldn't get that either, I suspect. And if this mandate holds up, EU users might lose AirDrop.

Mandating that the public has to be allowed to use the same doorways as a hotel's own staff doesn't mean those existing doors will be opened to everyone. It could lead to those doors being closed to everyone.
key_insight: The EU's interoperability mandate conflates forcing adoption of open standards with forcing companies to give away proprietary technology, and Apple's most likely response is to withhold features from EU customers rather than open its ecosystem.
